findings.description: Optional: Mandatory centralization of transition logic (Task 5.1). Centralizing the next-phase decision logic (D10) should be a mandatory architectural improvement for maintainability and auditability, rather than being contingent on whether the audit identifies Layer D as the specific cause of the recent failure.
findings.resolution: Update Task 5.1 to always extract and centralize the next-phase logic into a single auditable structure (table/dict) regardless of the audit's findings on the current implementation's correctness.

findings.description: Optional: Handle 'double-failure' scenarios in orchestrator (D9). If `apply-outcome` fails due to a system-level issue like a full disk or read-only filesystem, the orchestrator's attempt to transition to `ESCALATE` (which also requires a write to loop-state.json) will also fail.
findings.resolution: The orchestrator should be documented (or implemented) to emit a critical 'panic' message to stderr and preserve the handoff file if it cannot even write the state transition to ESCALATE.

findings.description: Optional: Mechanically detect unauthorized edits to loop-state.json (Layer C). While prompt-based prohibitions (D1) are a good first step, they rely on sub-agent compliance. A more resilient approach would be to verify the file hasn't changed unexpectedly.
findings.resolution: Consider adding a simple hash or mtime check on loop-state.json in the orchestrator. If the file is modified during sub-agent execution without an authorized `apply-outcome` call (or if the sub-agent bypassed the tool and edited directly), the orchestrator should detect the out-of-band change and transition to ESCALATE.

findings.description: High-quality shift to structured capability fields (D3). The decision to move away from brittle substring matching on system prompts in favor of a structured `write_capable` field significantly improves the system's technical integrity and prevents future role-confusion regressions.

Comprehensive 4-layer defense in depth (D1). The proposal correctly identifies that sub-agent shell behavior is not fully observable or enforceable via a single layer, and by addressing all four candidate pathways (A through D), it provides a robust fix for the observed transition bug.
